answer:
Three elegies in the 'Old English Elegies' collection (unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e) directly and substantively speak of the man-and-his-lord relation:

1. **The Wanderer (chapter 11)** — The most central treatment of the comitatus bond. The wanderer has lost his 'gold-friend' (lord) and wanders the ice-cold sea seeking a new treasure-giver. He recalls how his lord welcomed him to the feast and the gift-throne, and dreams of clasping and kissing 'his liege-lord.' The poem's entire sorrow flows from the rupture of the thane-lord bond.

2. **Deor (chapter 13)** — The closing stanza explicitly frames the poem as a scop's lament over losing his lord's favor: 'for a time I was the Heodenings' scop, dear to my lord… I had a goodly following, a loyal lord, until Heorrenda… received the land-right which the protector of earls had granted me before.' The man-and-lord bond, and its loss, is the poem's personal climax.

3. **Widsith (chapter 14)** — A wandering scop who 'followed many lords' across peoples and tribes. He describes his bond with his protecting lord Eadgils of the Myrgings (giving him the ring-gift of Eormanric as tribute) and the reciprocal exchange of song for ring-giving that structures every lord relationship in the poem.

Other elegies in the collection (e.g., The Seafarer, ch. 5; The Gifts of Men, ch. 1; The Fortunes of Men, ch. 2) mention the lord-thane relationship in passing, but it is not their primary elegiac concern.
